0: <title>Effect of handling and feeding strategies in the environmental performance of buffalo milk in Northeastern Brazil </title>
Bruna Borges Soares 1 . Edmar Costa Alves2 Henrique Leonardo Maranduba³ 3 Fabiano Ferreira da Silva Sérgio Augusto de Albuquerque Fernandes³ José Adolfo de Almeida Neto 4 Luciano Brito Rodrigues 3
Received: 6 October 2017 / Accepted: 27 October 2018 Published online: 12 November 2018 Springer-Verlag GmbH Germany, part of Springer Nature 2018
1: Purpose To assess the effect of intensification of feeding strategies on the environmental impacts of different animal-handling scenarios of buffalo milk production.

Methods Seven scenarios suitable for buffalo milk production were considered-baseline system (BS), S1, S2, S3, S4, S5, and S6-differing from one another in the management system, diet used, and productivity. An attributional cradle-to-farm gate life cycle assessment (LCA) was performed in a buffalo milk farm with a herd of 691 animals. The functional unit was 1 kg of fat (4%) and protein (3.3%) corrected milk (FPCM). The product system comprised four unit processes: mineral salt production, homeopathic medicine production, animal breeding, and milking and milk cooling. The impact assessment considered six categories from the ReCiPe 2016 midpoint method using the software SimaPro as a supporting tool for the modeling and impact assessment of scenarios.

Results and discussion The BS was the most impactful among the other scenarios in the categories "climate change (CC)," "land use (LU)," and "water consumption (WC)." S5 and S6 were the most impactful in the categories "terrestrial acidification (TA)," "freshwater eutrophication (FE)," and "fossil resource scarcity (FRS)."

Conclusions This study can help in choosing feed resources and the handling system and analyzing the consequences on the categories considering their impacts. However, the intensification of production should be achieved by improving both the quality of animal feeding and the management of cropping practices.
Keywords Dairy buffalo . Environmental management . Handling systems . LCA . Organic milk
Electronic supplementary material The online version of this article (https://doi.org/10.1007/s11367-018-1547-4) contains supplementary material, which is available to authorized users.

3: The Brazilian agribusiness chain is a relevant economic player in the international market. The country is one of the world's largest producers and exporters of soybean, maize, rice, and meat (poultry, bovine, swine) (OECD-FAO 2015).

Buffalo livestock has a high growth potential in national agricultural production, emerging as an attractive and profit- able alternative for both meat and milk products. Since the 1980s and 1990s, Brazilian buffalo farming has spread throughout the country, especially for milk production, be- cause of the buffalo's characteristics of adaptation and man- ageability when compared with other domestic ruminants (Bernardes 2007; Santos et al. 2016).

Despite the growing potential for buffalo livestock, the environmental impacts related to such activity cannot be de- nied. This is due to its consequences at various environmental levels, specifically in the release of greenhouse gas emissions

(GHG), and its association with climate change. According to the Food and Agriculture Organization (FAO) (2013), the total GHG emissions from buffalo production represent 9% of global emissions by animal species, corresponding to 618 million tons of CO2 equivalent. From this, 390 million tons of CO2 eq. are derived from milk production. Other impacts related to buffalo farming include abiotic depletion, terrestrial acidification, and freshwater eutrophication (Pirlo et al. 2014a). Therefore, this livestock system, nonorganic or organ- ic, requires environmental strategies and alternatives to im- prove its productivity and sustainability once buffalo milk production becomes important to global milk production (IDF 2009).

Life cycle assessment (LCA) is one of the methodological approaches that may identify and quantify the environmental impacts related to a product. LCA has been used to support the decision-making processes regarding food production and to minimize its associated effects. In this sense, the use of LCA in investigating agro-industrial products has increased in sev- eral areas, such as livestock systems.

While beef (Willers et al. 2017) and milk (Léis et al. 2015) cattle production LCA studies have been investigated, includ- ing in Brazil, buffalo milk production has received a closer examination only recently. The first studies were performed in Italy (Pirlo et al. 2012, 2014a, b; Sabia et al. 2018), India (Garg et al. 2016), and Romania (Popa et al. 2016).

According to literature review, Pirlo et al. (2012) performed the first LCA study of buffalo milk on two dairy farms in Southern Italy. Afterward, Pirlo et al. (2014b) studied the car- bon footprint of milk produced in six Mediterranean buffalo farms in Italy through LCA. Pirlo et al. (2014a) quantified the environmental impact of 1 kg of standardized buffalo milk (LBN) with the fat and protein content adjusted for milk of Mediterranean buffaloes in Italy. The study considered six farms, and the impact categories investigated were global warming, abiotic depletion, photochemical ozone formation, acidification, and eutrophication. Garg et al. (2016) assessed the carbon footprint of milk produced in multifunctional dairy systems in Western India by monitoring CO2, CH4, and N2O emissions from buffaloes. The emissions of CH4 from enteric fermentation were identified as the main hotspot in small dairy cattle and buffaloes. Popa et al. (2016) evaluated the methane emission from the enteric fermentation of cattle and buffalo in Romania from 1984 to 2014. Sabia et al. (2018) compared through LCA the environmental impact of two different rear- ing systems for buffalo heifers (pasture-based and indoor sys- items). Despite these studies contributing to buffalo milk pro- duction, gaps must still be investigated, such as those related to the environmental effects of different handling scenarios to reach better productivity.
Studies of intensification in other livestock production have been frequent in literature. Seó et al. (2017) summarized the main recent LCA studies in dairy cattle and identified that

the intensification affects the environmental performance of milk. The works of Woldegebriel et al. (2017) in Ethiopia, Bakken et al. (2017) in Norway, and Soteriades et al. (2016) in France are some of the latest.

Brazilian studies are needed to assess whether local inten- sification strategies contribute to improved environmental per- formances of livestock systems. Thus, before implementation, assessing the environmental consequences that make these scenarios less impactful and more sustainable is necessary. This study performed different animal handling and feed strat- legies for buffalo milk production to analyze the trade-offs of the environmental burdens.

8: An attributional LCA was performed according to ISO 14040 (2006a) and ISO 14044 (2006b) standards. Seven handling scenarios suitable for buffalo milk production were consid- ered. Primary data were collected from a farm in the center- south meso-region of Bahia, Brazil (Fig. 1).

The scenarios considered that the herd remained the same, changing the land required for pasture. The software SuperCrac yielded the nutritional requirements considering the weight and productivity of herds. This software allows feed formulations according to the production and composi- tion of the required milk (TDSoftware 2018). Thus, the fol- lowing scenarios were considered:

Baseline scenario (BS). The animal breeding is extensive, occurring in a nonirrigated organic pasture and supple- mented only with mineral salt, according to animal age-mature (mineral salt 1) and young (mineral salt 2). The grazing area comprises 547.31 ha, corresponding to 90% of the area for buffalo breeding. The pasture does not receive chemical pesticides or synthetic fertilizers and is predominantly composed of Brachiaria decumbens. The herd reproduces by natural mating, and its annual average size is composed of 691 buffaloes of pure Murrah breed and Murrah-Mediterranean, namely, 195 mature dairy buffaloes, 112 other mature (bulls, animal traction, and nonlactating buffaloes), and 384 growing buffaloes (heifers and calves). Surplus animals (calves buffaloes, heifers, and bulls) as unproductive dairy buffaloes are coproducts (meat) and sold for slaughter. The milking is performed under sanitary conditions using a mechanical process, with a calf at foot to stimulate milk release. Next, the milk is cooled to ~ 7 °C (45 °F) and stored in a tank until the delivery to a dairy industry 2 km from the farm. The average daily raw organic milk production is approx- imately 780 L/day (4.0 kg per animal/day), with 6.75% fat and 3.69% protein content. The baseline system has great relevance and is typical in the considered

Bicalcium phosphate (S1, S2, S3, S4, S5 and S6)
Limestone (S1, S2, S3, S4, S5 and S6)
Transport (S1, S2, S3, S4, S5 and S6)
Emissions to air CH4. N2O, NH3, NOx

- Land occupation [construction site] (S5 and S6)

<list>geographical region. This region is one of the most im- portant areas in Northeast Brazilian for agriculture and livestock because of its excellent soil and climate (Willers et al. 2017). 
Scenario 1 (S1) is like BS, but the animal breeding is changed to semi-intensive. Organic maize and soybean supplements purchased off-farm are added. The milk pro- duction is 8.0 kg/day per animal, with 6.8% fat content. 
Scenario 2 (S2) is like S1, but nonorganics (maize and soybean) replace the organic supplements. 
Scenario 3 (S3) is like S1, but whey replaces a part of organic supplements. 
- Scenario 4 (S4) is like S2, but whey replaces a part of nonorganic supplements. 
Scenario 5 (S5) is like BS, but the animal breeding is changed to intensive, and nonorganic maize silage and concentrated feed (maize and soybean) purchased off-farm replace the organic pasture. The milk 
Whey is an important byproduct of the dairy industry be- cause of the large amount produced and its nutritional compo- sition (Mollea et al. 2013). Consequently, it can be considered for several applications. In this study, the whey was used in liquid form (in natura) for animal feed. 
Scenario 6 (S6) is like S5, but the feed supplementation is changed to increase the milk production to 16.0 kg/day per animal, with 5.9% fat content. 
production is 12.0 kg/day per animal, with 6.2% fat content. 
Based on an economic allocation, the impacts upstream of whey were initially not considered. This is common when dairy industries do not use it to produce new foods (e.g., soft cheeses and dairy beverages) or even to separate industrially proteins, lactose, and SO on. Thus, once the whey loses its commercial value, it is given to animal breeders (Santos et al. 2017). Since the milk farm was 2 km from the dairy</list>
Permanent flow between Unit Processes [occurs in all scenarios] Conditional flow between Unit Processes [occurs in some scenarios]

industry, the study did not consider the impacts of the trans- port of whey. Other inputs used for feed supplementation were purchased in São Paulo, Brazil, approximately 1500 km from the dairy farm. Because of the amount of maize silage used in the in- tensive systems S5 and S6, a sensitivity analysis was per- formed for the transport of feed supplements. Hence, the anal- ysis considered that the purchasing occurs near the farm (S5A and S6A), with no distance, and in São Paulo (S5B and S6B).

The area for pasture was determined according to the con- sidered handling systems: extensive, BS (1 ha per animal); semi-intensive, S1 to S4 (0.4 ha per animal); and intensive, S5 and S6 (1.5E-03 ha per animal). As there are no pasture areas in S5 and S6, these scenarios considered the area occu- pied by the animal in the intensive production as a construc- tion site. The area formerly occupied by the pasture in the previous scenarios has been transformed into a nonuse natural area. The area required for producing feed supplements (maize and soybean) in S1 to S6 was considered.

The functional unit considered was 1 kg of fat and protein corrected milk (FPCM), which standardizes the energy con- tent to 4% fat and 3.3% protein. The system boundary comprises the unit process: homeo- pathic medicine production, mineral salt production, animal breeding, and milking and milk cooling. Regarding data quality requirements, we prioritized prima- ry data collected from the BS, corresponding to the 2015 pro- duction. If primary data were missing, data from ecoinvent V3.0 and Agri-footprint® V1.0 databases, literature, and the- oretical models were considered. They were used for BS and S1 to S6 inventories (Table 1). The electricity consumption and packaging in the mineral salt production unit process and water consumption in the animal breeding, for example, were obtained from the literature and extrapolated within 1 year. The materials used for fences were calculated based on the pasture area for each scenario, considering a useful life of 20 years for the wood and 75 years for the wire steel. It was considered that the wastewater resulting from milking process was sent to municipal sewage treatment plant. Buildings, infrastructure, equipment, and human work were not included in the system boundary. In addi- tion, compulsory vaccines and homeopathic compounds (used in the manufacture of medicines) were not included in the study because they contribute to less than 1% of the environmental impact (cutoff). The occupation of an existing pasture area for over 20 years accounted for the impacts due to land use change. As recommended by the International Dairy Federation (IDF) (2015), physical allocation method was used to share the environmental impacts of the main product (milk) with the coproducts (meat). This approach reflects the underlying use of feed energy by the dairy animals and the physiological feed requirements of the animal to produce milk and meat (IDF
2015). The allocation factors can be found in the supplemen- tary material (Table S1, Electronic Supplementary Material).

The direct emissions due to enteric fermentation, excreta volatilization, nitrate leaching, and NH3 volatilization were modeled following the guidelines for national greenhouse gas inventories from the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC 2006a, b). Enteric fermentation emissions were calculated from the Tier 2 method.

The amount of phosphorus in the chemical composition of manure is too discrete 1.6kg/ton) (De Vries et al. 2012) SO its emissions were neglected. However, the phosphorus emis- sions from the secondary processes were indirectly accounted for.

Six midpoint impact categories were considered for this study: climate change (CC, in kg CO2 eq.), terrestrial acidifi- cation (TA, in kg SO2 eq.), freshwater eutrophication (FE, in kg P eq.), land use (LU, in m² year crop eq.), water con- sumption (WC, in m³), and fossil resource scarcity (FRS, in kg oil eq.).

The characterization factors of the ReCiPe 2016 method (Huijbregts et al. 2016), an update from ReCiPe 2008 (Goedkoop et al. 2009) version 1.12, were used to create a correlation between the data obtained in the inventory and the environmental impacts associated with the elementary flows. SimaPro PhD version 8.5.2.0 was used for processing data and modeling the potential environmental impacts of the han- dling scenarios. In this update, the categories "agricultural land occupation" and "natural land transformation" were merged in the category "land use," which considers the com- plete cycle of land, including transformation, occupation, and relaxation (Huijbregts et al. 2016). The inputs-outputs and their corresponding database processes used for modeling the product system are shown in the supplementary material (Table S2, Electronic Supplementary Material).
9: An attributional LCA was performed according to ISO 14040 (2006a) and ISO 14044 (2006b) standards. Seven handling scenarios suitable for buffalo milk production were consid- ered. Primary data were collected from a farm in the center- south meso-region of Bahia, Brazil (Fig. 1).

The scenarios considered that the herd remained the same, changing the land required for pasture. The software SuperCrac yielded the nutritional requirements considering the weight and productivity of herds. This software allows feed formulations according to the production and composi- tion of the required milk (TDSoftware 2018). Thus, the fol- lowing scenarios were considered:

Baseline scenario (BS). The animal breeding is extensive, occurring in a nonirrigated organic pasture and supple- mented only with mineral salt, according to animal age-mature (mineral salt 1) and young (mineral salt 2). The grazing area comprises 547.31 ha, corresponding to 90% of the area for buffalo breeding. The pasture does not receive chemical pesticides or synthetic fertilizers and is predominantly composed of Brachiaria decumbens. The herd reproduces by natural mating, and its annual average size is composed of 691 buffaloes of pure Murrah breed and Murrah-Mediterranean, namely, 195 mature dairy buffaloes, 112 other mature (bulls, animal traction, and nonlactating buffaloes), and 384 growing buffaloes (heifers and calves). Surplus animals (calves buffaloes, heifers, and bulls) as unproductive dairy buffaloes are coproducts (meat) and sold for slaughter. The milking is performed under sanitary conditions using a mechanical process, with a calf at foot to stimulate milk release. Next, the milk is cooled to ~ 7 °C (45 °F) and stored in a tank until the delivery to a dairy industry 2 km from the farm. The average daily raw organic milk production is approx- imately 780 L/day (4.0 kg per animal/day), with 6.75% fat and 3.69% protein content. The baseline system has great relevance and is typical in the considered
Maize grain non-organic (S2, S4, S5 and S6)
Soybean non-organic ( S2, S4, S5 and S6)
Bicalcium phosphate (S1, S2, S3, S4, S5 and S6)
Limestone (S1, S2, S3, S4, S5 and S6)
Transport (S1, S2, S3, S4, S5 and S6)
Emissions to air CH4. N2O, NH3, NOx
Surplus buffaloes: calves, heifers and bulls

- Land occupation [construction site] (S5 and S6) 
- Land transformation [pasture for non-use] (S5 and S6)</list>

Scenario 1 (S1) is like BS, but the animal breeding is changed to semi-intensive. Organic maize and soybean supplements purchased off-farm are added. The milk pro- duction is 8.0 kg/day per animal, with 6.8% fat content. 
Scenario 2 (S2) is like S1, but nonorganics (maize and soybean) replace the organic supplements. 
Scenario 3 (S3) is like S1, but whey replaces a part of organic supplements. 
- Scenario 4 (S4) is like S2, but whey replaces a part of nonorganic supplements. 
Scenario 5 (S5) is like BS, but the animal breeding is changed to intensive, and nonorganic maize silage and concentrated feed (maize and soybean) purchased off-farm replace the organic pasture. The milk 
Whey is an important byproduct of the dairy industry be- cause of the large amount produced and its nutritional compo- sition (Mollea et al. 2013). Consequently, it can be considered for several applications. In this study, the whey was used in liquid form (in natura) for animal feed. 
Scenario 6 (S6) is like S5, but the feed supplementation is changed to increase the milk production to 16.0 kg/day per animal, with 5.9% fat content. 
production is 12.0 kg/day per animal, with 6.2% fat content. 
Based on an economic allocation, the impacts upstream of whey were initially not considered. This is common when dairy industries do not use it to produce new foods (e.g., soft cheeses and dairy beverages) or even to separate industrially proteins, lactose, and SO on. Thus, once the whey loses its commercial value, it is given to animal breeders (Santos et al. 2017). Since the milk farm was 2 km from the dairy</list>
Permanent flow between Unit Processes [occurs in all scenarios] Conditional flow between Unit Processes [occurs in some scenarios]

industry, the study did not consider the impacts of the trans- port of whey. Other inputs used for feed supplementation were purchased in São Paulo, Brazil, approximately 1500 km from the dairy farm. Because of the amount of maize silage used in the in- tensive systems S5 and S6, a sensitivity analysis was per- formed for the transport of feed supplements. Hence, the anal- ysis considered that the purchasing occurs near the farm (S5A and S6A), with no distance, and in São Paulo (S5B and S6B).

The area for pasture was determined according to the con- sidered handling systems: extensive, BS (1 ha per animal); semi-intensive, S1 to S4 (0.4 ha per animal); and intensive, S5 and S6 (1.5E-03 ha per animal). As there are no pasture areas in S5 and S6, these scenarios considered the area occu- pied by the animal in the intensive production as a construc- tion site. The area formerly occupied by the pasture in the previous scenarios has been transformed into a nonuse natural area. The area required for producing feed supplements (maize and soybean) in S1 to S6 was considered.

The functional unit considered was 1 kg of fat and protein corrected milk (FPCM), which standardizes the energy con- tent to 4% fat and 3.3% protein. <list>geographical region. This region is one of the most im- portant areas in Northeast Brazilian for agriculture and livestock because of its excellent soil and climate (Willers et al. 2017). 
Scenario 1 (S1) is like BS, but the animal breeding is changed to semi-intensive. Organic maize and soybean supplements purchased off-farm are added. The milk pro- duction is 8.0 kg/day per animal, with 6.8% fat content. 
Scenario 2 (S2) is like S1, but nonorganics (maize and soybean) replace the organic supplements. 
Scenario 3 (S3) is like S1, but whey replaces a part of organic supplements. 
- Scenario 4 (S4) is like S2, but whey replaces a part of nonorganic supplements. 
Scenario 5 (S5) is like BS, but the animal breeding is changed to intensive, and nonorganic maize silage and concentrated feed (maize and soybean) purchased off-farm replace the organic pasture. The milk 
Whey is an important byproduct of the dairy industry be- cause of the large amount produced and its nutritional compo- sition (Mollea et al. 2013). Consequently, it can be considered for several applications. In this study, the whey was used in liquid form (in natura) for animal feed. 
Scenario 6 (S6) is like S5, but the feed supplementation is changed to increase the milk production to 16.0 kg/day per animal, with 5.9% fat content. 
production is 12.0 kg/day per animal, with 6.2% fat content. 
Based on an economic allocation, the impacts upstream of whey were initially not considered. This is common when dairy industries do not use it to produce new foods (e.g., soft cheeses and dairy beverages) or even to separate industrially proteins, lactose, and SO on. Thus, once the whey loses its commercial value, it is given to animal breeders (Santos et al. 2017). Since the milk farm was 2 km from the dairy</list>
Permanent flow between Unit Processes [occurs in all scenarios] Conditional flow between Unit Processes [occurs in some scenarios]

industry, the study did not consider the impacts of the trans- port of whey. Other inputs used for feed supplementation were purchased in São Paulo, Brazil, approximately 1500 km from the dairy farm. Because of the amount of maize silage used in the in- tensive systems S5 and S6, a sensitivity analysis was per- formed for the transport of feed supplements. Hence, the anal- ysis considered that the purchasing occurs near the farm (S5A and S6A), with no distance, and in São Paulo (S5B and S6B).

The area for pasture was determined according to the con- sidered handling systems: extensive, BS (1 ha per animal); semi-intensive, S1 to S4 (0.4 ha per animal); and intensive, S5 and S6 (1.5E-03 ha per animal). As there are no pasture areas in S5 and S6, these scenarios considered the area occu- pied by the animal in the intensive production as a construc- tion site. The area formerly occupied by the pasture in the previous scenarios has been transformed into a nonuse natural area. The area required for producing feed supplements (maize and soybean) in S1 to S6 was considered.

The functional unit considered was 1 kg of fat and protein corrected milk (FPCM), which standardizes the energy con- tent to 4% fat and 3.3% protein. The system boundary comprises the unit process: homeo- pathic medicine production, mineral salt production, animal breeding, and milking and milk cooling. Regarding data quality requirements, we prioritized prima- ry data collected from the BS, corresponding to the 2015 pro- duction. If primary data were missing, data from ecoinvent V3.0 and Agri-footprint® V1.0 databases, literature, and the- oretical models were considered. They were used for BS and S1 to S6 inventories (Table 1). The electricity consumption and packaging in the mineral salt production unit process and water consumption in the animal breeding, for example, were obtained from the literature and extrapolated within 1 year. The materials used for fences were calculated based on the pasture area for each scenario, considering a useful life of 20 years for the wood and 75 years for the wire steel. It was considered that the wastewater resulting from milking process was sent to municipal sewage treatment plant. Buildings, infrastructure, equipment, and human work were not included in the system boundary. In addi- tion, compulsory vaccines and homeopathic compounds (used in the manufacture of medicines) were not included in the study because they contribute to less than 1% of the environmental impact (cutoff). The occupation of an existing pasture area for over 20 years accounted for the impacts due to land use change. As recommended by the International Dairy Federation (IDF) (2015), physical allocation method was used to share the environmental impacts of the main product (milk) with the coproducts (meat). This approach reflects the underlying use of feed energy by the dairy animals and the physiological feed requirements of the animal to produce milk and meat (IDF
2015). The allocation factors can be found in the supplemen- tary material (Table S1, Electronic Supplementary Material).

The direct emissions due to enteric fermentation, excreta volatilization, nitrate leaching, and NH3 volatilization were modeled following the guidelines for national greenhouse gas inventories from the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC 2006a, b). Enteric fermentation emissions were calculated from the Tier 2 method.

The amount of phosphorus in the chemical composition of manure is too discrete 1.6kg/ton) (De Vries et al. 2012) SO its emissions were neglected. However, the phosphorus emis- sions from the secondary processes were indirectly accounted for.

Six midpoint impact categories were considered for this study: climate change (CC, in kg CO2 eq.), terrestrial acidifi- cation (TA, in kg SO2 eq.), freshwater eutrophication (FE, in kg P eq.), land use (LU, in m² year crop eq.), water con- sumption (WC, in m³), and fossil resource scarcity (FRS, in kg oil eq.).

The characterization factors of the ReCiPe 2016 method (Huijbregts et al. 2016), an update from ReCiPe 2008 (Goedkoop et al. 2009) version 1.12, were used to create a correlation between the data obtained in the inventory and the environmental impacts associated with the elementary flows. SimaPro PhD version 8.5.2.0 was used for processing data and modeling the potential environmental impacts of the han- dling scenarios. In this update, the categories "agricultural land occupation" and "natural land transformation" were merged in the category "land use," which considers the com- plete cycle of land, including transformation, occupation, and relaxation (Huijbregts et al. 2016). The inputs-outputs and their corresponding database processes used for modeling the product system are shown in the supplementary material (Table S2, Electronic Supplementary Material).
13: An attributional LCA was performed according to ISO 14040 (2006a) and ISO 14044 (2006b) standards. Seven handling scenarios suitable for buffalo milk production were consid- ered. Primary data were collected from a farm in the center- south meso-region of Bahia, Brazil (Fig. 1).

The scenarios considered that the herd remained the same, changing the land required for pasture. The software SuperCrac yielded the nutritional requirements considering the weight and productivity of herds. This software allows feed formulations according to the production and composi- tion of the required milk (TDSoftware 2018). Thus, the fol- lowing scenarios were considered:

Baseline scenario (BS). The animal breeding is extensive, occurring in a nonirrigated organic pasture and supple- mented only with mineral salt, according to animal age-mature (mineral salt 1) and young (mineral salt 2). The grazing area comprises 547.31 ha, corresponding to 90% of the area for buffalo breeding. The pasture does not receive chemical pesticides or synthetic fertilizers and is predominantly composed of Brachiaria decumbens. The herd reproduces by natural mating, and its annual average size is composed of 691 buffaloes of pure Murrah breed and Murrah-Mediterranean, namely, 195 mature dairy buffaloes, 112 other mature (bulls, animal traction, and nonlactating buffaloes), and 384 growing buffaloes (heifers and calves). Surplus animals (calves buffaloes, heifers, and bulls) as unproductive dairy buffaloes are coproducts (meat) and sold for slaughter. The milking is performed under sanitary conditions using a mechanical process, with a calf at foot to stimulate milk release. Next, the milk is cooled to ~ 7 °C (45 °F) and stored in a tank until the delivery to a dairy industry 2 km from the farm. The average daily raw organic milk production is approx- imately 780 L/day (4.0 kg per animal/day), with 6.75% fat and 3.69% protein content. The baseline system has great relevance and is typical in the considered
Maize grain non-organic (S2, S4, S5 and S6)
Soybean non-organic ( S2, S4, S5 and S6)
Bicalcium phosphate (S1, S2, S3, S4, S5 and S6)
Limestone (S1, S2, S3, S4, S5 and S6)
Transport (S1, S2, S3, S4, S5 and S6)
Emissions to air CH4. N2O, NH3, NOx
Surplus buffaloes: calves, heifers and bulls

The electricity consumption and packaging in the mineral salt production unit process and water consumption in the animal breeding, for example, were obtained from the literature and extrapolated within 1 year. The materials used for fences were calculated based on the pasture area for each scenario, considering a useful life of 20 years for the wood and 75 years for the wire steel. It was considered that the wastewater resulting from milking process was sent to municipal sewage treatment plant. Buildings, infrastructure, equipment, and human work were not included in the system boundary. In addi- tion, compulsory vaccines and homeopathic compounds (used in the manufacture of medicines) were not included in the study because they contribute to less than 1% of the environmental impact (cutoff). The occupation of an existing pasture area for over 20 years accounted for the impacts due to land use change. As recommended by the International Dairy Federation (IDF) (2015), physical allocation method was used to share the environmental impacts of the main product (milk) with the coproducts (meat). This approach reflects the underlying use of feed energy by the dairy animals and the physiological feed requirements of the animal to produce milk and meat (IDF
2015). The allocation factors can be found in the supplemen- tary material (Table S1, Electronic Supplementary Material).

The direct emissions due to enteric fermentation, excreta volatilization, nitrate leaching, and NH3 volatilization were modeled following the guidelines for national greenhouse gas inventories from the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC 2006a, b). Enteric fermentation emissions were calculated from the Tier 2 method.

The amount of phosphorus in the chemical composition of manure is too discrete 1.6kg/ton) (De Vries et al. 2012) SO its emissions were neglected. However, the phosphorus emis- sions from the secondary processes were indirectly accounted for.

Six midpoint impact categories were considered for this study: climate change (CC, in kg CO2 eq.), terrestrial acidifi- cation (TA, in kg SO2 eq.), freshwater eutrophication (FE, in kg P eq.), land use (LU, in m² year crop eq.), water con- sumption (WC, in m³), and fossil resource scarcity (FRS, in kg oil eq.).

The characterization factors of the ReCiPe 2016 method (Huijbregts et al. 2016), an update from ReCiPe 2008 (Goedkoop et al. 2009) version 1.12, were used to create a correlation between the data obtained in the inventory and the environmental impacts associated with the elementary flows. SimaPro PhD version 8.5.2.0 was used for processing data and modeling the potential environmental impacts of the han- dling scenarios. In this update, the categories "agricultural land occupation" and "natural land transformation" were merged in the category "land use," which considers the com- plete cycle of land, including transformation, occupation, and relaxation (Huijbregts et al. 2016). The inputs-outputs and their corresponding database processes used for modeling the product system are shown in the supplementary material (Table S2, Electronic Supplementary Material).
16: An attributional LCA was performed according to ISO 14040 (2006a) and ISO 14044 (2006b) standards. Seven handling scenarios suitable for buffalo milk production were consid- ered. Primary data were collected from a farm in the center- south meso-region of Bahia, Brazil (Fig. 1).

The scenarios considered that the herd remained the same, changing the land required for pasture. The software SuperCrac yielded the nutritional requirements considering the weight and productivity of herds. This software allows feed formulations according to the production and composi- tion of the required milk (TDSoftware 2018). Thus, the fol- lowing scenarios were considered:

Baseline scenario (BS). The animal breeding is extensive, occurring in a nonirrigated organic pasture and supple- mented only with mineral salt, according to animal age-mature (mineral salt 1) and young (mineral salt 2). The grazing area comprises 547.31 ha, corresponding to 90% of the area for buffalo breeding. The pasture does not receive chemical pesticides or synthetic fertilizers and is predominantly composed of Brachiaria decumbens. The herd reproduces by natural mating, and its annual average size is composed of 691 buffaloes of pure Murrah breed and Murrah-Mediterranean, namely, 195 mature dairy buffaloes, 112 other mature (bulls, animal traction, and nonlactating buffaloes), and 384 growing buffaloes (heifers and calves). Surplus animals (calves buffaloes, heifers, and bulls) as unproductive dairy buffaloes are coproducts (meat) and sold for slaughter. The milking is performed under sanitary conditions using a mechanical process, with a calf at foot to stimulate milk release. Next, the milk is cooled to ~ 7 °C (45 °F) and stored in a tank until the delivery to a dairy industry 2 km from the farm. The average daily raw organic milk production is approx- imately 780 L/day (4.0 kg per animal/day), with 6.75% fat and 3.69% protein content. The baseline system has great relevance and is typical in the considered

Scenario 5 (S5) is like BS, but the animal breeding is changed to intensive, and nonorganic maize silage and concentrated feed (maize and soybean) purchased off-farm replace the organic pasture. The milk 
Whey is an important byproduct of the dairy industry be- cause of the large amount produced and its nutritional compo- sition (Mollea et al. 2013). Consequently, it can be considered for several applications. In this study, the whey was used in liquid form (in natura) for animal feed. 
Scenario 6 (S6) is like S5, but the feed supplementation is changed to increase the milk production to 16.0 kg/day per animal, with 5.9% fat content.

Based on an economic allocation, the impacts upstream of whey were initially not considered. This is common when dairy industries do not use it to produce new foods (e.g., soft cheeses and dairy beverages) or even to separate industrially proteins, lactose, and SO on. Thus, once the whey loses its commercial value, it is given to animal breeders (Santos et al. 2017). Since the milk farm was 2 km from the dairy</list>

industry, the study did not consider the impacts of the trans- port of whey. Other inputs used for feed supplementation were purchased in São Paulo, Brazil, approximately 1500 km from the dairy farm. Because of the amount of maize silage used in the in- tensive systems S5 and S6, a sensitivity analysis was per- formed for the transport of feed supplements. Hence, the anal- ysis considered that the purchasing occurs near the farm (S5A and S6A), with no distance, and in São Paulo (S5B and S6B).

The area for pasture was determined according to the con- sidered handling systems: extensive, BS (1 ha per animal); semi-intensive, S1 to S4 (0.4 ha per animal); and intensive, S5 and S6 (1.5E-03 ha per animal). As there are no pasture areas in S5 and S6, these scenarios considered the area occu- pied by the animal in the intensive production as a construc- tion site. The area formerly occupied by the pasture in the previous scenarios has been transformed into a nonuse natural area. The area required for producing feed supplements (maize and soybean) in S1 to S6 was considered.

The functional unit considered was 1 kg of fat and protein corrected milk (FPCM), which standardizes the energy con- tent to 4% fat and 3.3% protein. The system boundary comprises the unit process: homeo- pathic medicine production, mineral salt production, animal breeding, and milking and milk cooling. Regarding data quality requirements, we prioritized prima- ry data collected from the BS, corresponding to the 2015 pro- duction. If primary data were missing, data from ecoinvent V3.0 and Agri-footprint® V1.0 databases, literature, and the- oretical models were considered. They were used for BS and S1 to S6 inventories (Table 1). The electricity consumption and packaging in the mineral salt production unit process and water consumption in the animal breeding, for example, were obtained from the literature and extrapolated within 1 year. The materials used for fences were calculated based on the pasture area for each scenario, considering a useful life of 20 years for the wood and 75 years for the wire steel. It was considered that the wastewater resulting from milking process was sent to municipal sewage treatment plant. Buildings, infrastructure, equipment, and human work were not included in the system boundary. In addi- tion, compulsory vaccines and homeopathic compounds (used in the manufacture of medicines) were not included in the study because they contribute to less than 1% of the environmental impact (cutoff). The occupation of an existing pasture area for over 20 years accounted for the impacts due to land use change. As recommended by the International Dairy Federation (IDF) (2015), physical allocation method was used to share the environmental impacts of the main product (milk) with the coproducts (meat). This approach reflects the underlying use of feed energy by the dairy animals and the physiological feed requirements of the animal to produce milk and meat (IDF
2015). The allocation factors can be found in the supplemen- tary material (Table S1, Electronic Supplementary Material).

The direct emissions due to enteric fermentation, excreta volatilization, nitrate leaching, and NH3 volatilization were modeled following the guidelines for national greenhouse gas inventories from the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC 2006a, b). Enteric fermentation emissions were calculated from the Tier 2 method.

The amount of phosphorus in the chemical composition of manure is too discrete 1.6kg/ton) (De Vries et al. 2012) SO its emissions were neglected. However, the phosphorus emis- sions from the secondary processes were indirectly accounted for.

Six midpoint impact categories were considered for this study: climate change (CC, in kg CO2 eq.), terrestrial acidifi- cation (TA, in kg SO2 eq.), freshwater eutrophication (FE, in kg P eq.), land use (LU, in m² year crop eq.), water con- sumption (WC, in m³), and fossil resource scarcity (FRS, in kg oil eq.).

The characterization factors of the ReCiPe 2016 method (Huijbregts et al. 2016), an update from ReCiPe 2008 (Goedkoop et al. 2009) version 1.12, were used to create a correlation between the data obtained in the inventory and the environmental impacts associated with the elementary flows. SimaPro PhD version 8.5.2.0 was used for processing data and modeling the potential environmental impacts of the han- dling scenarios. In this update, the categories "agricultural land occupation" and "natural land transformation" were merged in the category "land use," which considers the com- plete cycle of land, including transformation, occupation, and relaxation (Huijbregts et al. 2016). 17: The environmental impacts of buffalo milk from BS and other scenarios proposed are shown in Fig. 2, and their absolute values are included in the supplementary material (Table S3, Electronic Supplementary Material).
18: The animal breeding was the unit process that most contrib- uted to the environmental impacts in the categories CC, TA, LU, and WC. In CC, the impacts reached 98.53%, with meth- ane biogenic (81.94%) and dinitrogen monoxide (16.46%) from the enteric fermentation and manure as the main contributors.
The enteric emissions are related to the animal's body weight, growth rate, and milk production (IPCC 2006a).

Fig. 2 Impact assessment of raw buffalo milk production for different scenarios based on the midpoint impact indicators from the ReCiPe 2016 (H) V1.02/World Recipe H/Characterization method

In TA, the ammonia (NH3) released from manure was the main contributor with 58.2%, while the N2O showed 13.75%. In LU, 99.86% of the impacts resulted from the large area required for animal grazing, a predominant characteristic in the extensive system. In WC, 97.19% of the impacts occurred because of the water required for watering.

The milking and milk cooling was the most impactful unit process in FE (98.84%) and FRS (84.73%). In FE, the effluent sent to treatment (wastewater treatment) contributed with 39.58%, followed by electricity (38.73%) and the predipping iodine used for cleaning the buffalo teats before milking (10.94%). For FRS, the main contributors were electricity (39.22%) and the predipping iodine (25.43%).

The Brazilian energy matrix comprises a mix of energy sources, with a predominance of hydroelectric power but with important participation of natural gas, which is responsible for the impacts of FE and FRS.

a Wastewater purified in a small size municipal wastewater treatment plant (capacity class 5), with an average capacity size of 806 per capita-equivalents (PCE)
Microbiological nitrification and denitrification of N com- pounds in manure excreted in the grassland (IPCC 2006b) produced the direct and indirect N20 emissions.
The contribution of the GHG emissions from land use change derived from the paper towel used in the teats cleaning (83.33%) and wood fences (19.13%).
CC - climate change, TA - terrestrial acidification, FE - freshwater eutrophication, LU - land use, FRS fossil resource scarcity, WC - water consumption.
Fig. 2 Impact assessment of raw buffalo milk production for different scenarios based on the midpoint impact indicators from the ReCiPe 2016 (H) V1.02/World Recipe H/Characterization method

In TA, the ammonia (NH3) released from manure was the main contributor with 58.2%, while the N2O showed 13.75%. In LU, 99.86% of the impacts resulted from the large area required for animal grazing, a predominant characteristic in the extensive system. In WC, 97.19% of the impacts occurred because of the water required for watering.

The milking and milk cooling was the most impactful unit process in FE (98.84%) and FRS (84.73%). In FE, the effluent sent to treatment (wastewater treatment) contributed with 39.58%, followed by electricity (38.73%) and the predipping iodine used for cleaning the buffalo teats before milking (10.94%). For FRS, the main contributors were electricity (39.22%) and the predipping iodine (25.43%).

The Brazilian energy matrix comprises a mix of energy sources, with a predominance of hydroelectric power but with important participation of natural gas, which is responsible for the impacts of FE and FRS.

Microbiological nitrification and denitrification of N com- pounds in manure excreted in the grassland (IPCC 2006b) produced the direct and indirect N20 emissions.
The contribution of the GHG emissions from land use change derived from the paper towel used in the teats cleaning (83.33%) and wood fences (19.13%).
CC - climate change, TA - terrestrial acidification, FE - freshwater eutrophication, LU - land use, FRS fossil resource scarcity, WC - water consumption.
Fig. 2 Impact assessment of raw buffalo milk production for different scenarios based on the midpoint impact indicators from the ReCiPe 2016 (H) V1.02/World Recipe H/Characterization method

In TA, the ammonia (NH3) released from manure was the main contributor with 58.2%, while the N2O showed 13.75%. In LU, 99.86% of the impacts resulted from the large area required for animal grazing, a predominant characteristic in the extensive system. In WC, 97.19% of the impacts occurred because of the water required for watering.

The milking and milk cooling was the most impactful unit process in FE (98.84%) and FRS (84.73%). In FE, the effluent sent to treatment (wastewater treatment) contributed with 39.58%, followed by electricity (38.73%) and the predipping iodine used for cleaning the buffalo teats before milking (10.94%). For FRS, the main contributors were electricity (39.22%) and the predipping iodine (25.43%).

The Brazilian energy matrix comprises a mix of energy sources, with a predominance of hydroelectric power but with important participation of natural gas, which is responsible for the impacts of FE and FRS.

Microbiological nitrification and denitrification of N com- pounds in manure excreted in the grassland (IPCC 2006b) produced the direct and indirect N20 emissions.

Fig. 2 Impact assessment of raw buffalo milk production for different scenarios based on the midpoint impact indicators from the ReCiPe 2016 (H) V1.02/World Recipe H/Characterization method

In TA, the ammonia (NH3) released from manure was the main contributor with 58.2%, while the N2O showed 13.75%. In LU, 99.86% of the impacts resulted from the large area required for animal grazing, a predominant characteristic in the extensive system. In WC, 97.19% of the impacts occurred because of the water required for watering.

The milking and milk cooling was the most impactful unit process in FE (98.84%) and FRS (84.73%). In FE, the effluent sent to treatment (wastewater treatment) contributed with 39.58%, followed by electricity (38.73%) and the predipping iodine used for cleaning the buffalo teats before milking (10.94%). For FRS, the main contributors were electricity (39.22%) and the predipping iodine (25.43%).

The Brazilian energy matrix comprises a mix of energy sources, with a predominance of hydroelectric power but with important participation of natural gas, which is responsible for the impacts of FE and FRS.

Microbiological nitrification and denitrification of N com- pounds in manure excreted in the grassland (IPCC 2006b) produced the direct and indirect N20 emissions.

Fig. 2 Impact assessment of raw buffalo milk production for different scenarios based on the midpoint impact indicators from the ReCiPe 2016 (H) V1.02/World Recipe H/Characterization method

In TA, the ammonia (NH3) released from manure was the main contributor with 58.2%, while the N2O showed 13.75%. In LU, 99.86% of the impacts resulted from the large area required for animal grazing, a predominant characteristic in the extensive system. In WC, 97.19% of the impacts occurred because of the water required for watering.

The milking and milk cooling was the most impactful unit process in FE (98.84%) and FRS (84.73%). In FE, the effluent sent to treatment (wastewater treatment) contributed with 39.58%, followed by electricity (38.73%) and the predipping iodine used for cleaning the buffalo teats before milking (10.94%). For FRS, the main contributors were electricity (39.22%) and the predipping iodine (25.43%).

The Brazilian energy matrix comprises a mix of energy sources, with a predominance of hydroelectric power but with important participation of natural gas, which is responsible for the impacts of FE and FRS.

a Wastewater purified in a small size municipal wastewater treatment plant (capacity class 5), with an average capacity size of 806 per capita-equivalents (PCE)
Microbiological nitrification and denitrification of N com- pounds in manure excreted in the grassland (IPCC 2006b) produced the direct and indirect N20 emissions.
The contribution of the GHG emissions from land use change derived from the paper towel used in the teats cleaning (83.33%) and wood fences (19.13%).
CC - climate change, TA - terrestrial acidification, FE - freshwater eutrophication, LU - land use, FRS fossil resource scarcity, WC - water consumption.
Fig. 2 Impact assessment of raw buffalo milk production for different scenarios based on the midpoint impact indicators from the ReCiPe 2016 (H) V1.02/World Recipe H/Characterization method

In TA, the ammonia (NH3) released from manure was the main contributor with 58.2%, while the N2O showed 13.75%. In LU, 99.86% of the impacts resulted from the large area required for animal grazing, a predominant characteristic in the extensive system. In WC, 97.19% of the impacts occurred because of the water required for watering.

The milking and milk cooling was the most impactful unit process in FE (98.84%) and FRS (84.73%). In FE, the effluent sent to treatment (wastewater treatment) contributed with 39.58%, followed by electricity (38.73%) and the predipping iodine used for cleaning the buffalo teats before milking (10.94%). For FRS, the main contributors were electricity (39.22%) and the predipping iodine (25.43%).

The Brazilian energy matrix comprises a mix of energy sources, with a predominance of hydroelectric power but with important participation of natural gas, which is responsible for the impacts of FE and FRS.

a Wastewater purified in a small size municipal wastewater treatment plant (capacity class 5), with an average capacity size of 806 per capita-equivalents (PCE)
Microbiological nitrification and denitrification of N com- pounds in manure excreted in the grassland (IPCC 2006b) produced the direct and indirect N20 emissions.
The contribution of the GHG emissions from land use change derived from the paper towel used in the teats cleaning (83.33%) and wood fences (19.13%).
CC - climate change, TA - terrestrial acidification, FE - freshwater eutrophication, LU - land use, FRS fossil resource scarcity, WC - water consumption.
Fig. 2 Impact assessment of raw buffalo milk production for different scenarios based on the midpoint impact indicators from the ReCiPe 2016 (H) V1.02/World Recipe H/Characterization method

In TA, the ammonia (NH3) released from manure was the main contributor with 58.2%, while the N2O showed 13.75%. In LU, 99.86% of the impacts resulted from the large area required for animal grazing, a predominant characteristic in the extensive system. In WC, 97.19% of the impacts occurred because of the water required for watering.

The milking and milk cooling was the most impactful unit process in FE (98.84%) and FRS (84.73%). In FE, the effluent sent to treatment (wastewater treatment) contributed with 39.58%, followed by electricity (38.73%) and the predipping iodine used for cleaning the buffalo teats before milking (10.94%). For FRS, the main contributors were electricity (39.22%) and the predipping iodine (25.43%).

The Brazilian energy matrix comprises a mix of energy sources, with a predominance of hydroelectric power but with important participation of natural gas, which is responsible for the impacts of FE and FRS.

a Wastewater purified in a small size municipal wastewater treatment plant (capacity class 5), with an average capacity size of 806 per capita-equivalents (PCE)
Microbiological nitrification and denitrification of N com- pounds in manure excreted in the grassland (IPCC 2006b) produced the direct and indirect N20 emissions.
The contribution of the GHG emissions from land use change derived from the paper towel used in the teats cleaning (83.33%) and wood fences (19.13%).
CC - climate change, TA - terrestrial acidification, FE - freshwater eutrophication, LU - land use, FRS fossil resource scarcity, WC - water consumption.
Fig. 2 Impact assessment of raw buffalo milk production for different scenarios based on the midpoint impact indicators from the ReCiPe 2016 (H) V1.02/World Recipe H/Characterization method

In TA, the ammonia (NH3) released from manure was the main contributor with 58.2%, while the N2O showed 13.75%. In LU, 99.86% of the impacts resulted from the large area required for animal grazing, a predominant characteristic in the extensive system. In WC, 97.19% of the impacts occurred because of the water required for watering.

The milking and milk cooling was the most impactful unit process in FE (98.84%) and FRS (84.73%). In FE, the effluent sent to treatment (wastewater treatment) contributed with 39.58%, followed by electricity (38.73%) and the predipping iodine used for cleaning the buffalo teats before milking (10.94%). For FRS, the main contributors were electricity (39.22%) and the predipping iodine (25.43%).

The Brazilian energy matrix comprises a mix of energy sources, with a predominance of hydroelectric power but with important participation of natural gas, which is responsible for the impacts of FE and FRS.

Fig. 2 Impact assessment of raw buffalo milk production for different scenarios based on the midpoint impact indicators from the ReCiPe 2016 (H) V1.02/World Recipe H/Characterization method

In TA, the ammonia (NH3) released from manure was the main contributor with 58.2%, while the N2O showed 13.75%. In LU, 99.86% of the impacts resulted from the large area required for animal grazing, a predominant characteristic in the extensive system. In WC, 97.19% of the impacts occurred because of the water required for watering.

The milking and milk cooling was the most impactful unit process in FE (98.84%) and FRS (84.73%). In FE, the effluent sent to treatment (wastewater treatment) contributed with 39.58%, followed by electricity (38.73%) and the predipping iodine used for cleaning the buffalo teats before milking (10.94%). For FRS, the main contributors were electricity (39.22%) and the predipping iodine (25.43%).

The Brazilian energy matrix comprises a mix of energy sources, with a predominance of hydroelectric power but with important participation of natural gas, which is responsible for the impacts of FE and FRS.

35: S1 (organic) and S2 (nonorganic) assessed the semi-intensive system for buffalo milk production. Compared with BS in CC, S1 and S2 presented a quite similar decrease in GHG emis- sions (62.95% and 62.70%, respectively). This behavior also occurred in LU, where both scenarios showed a decrease of

86.20% related to BS. Both scenarios presented a worse per- formance compared with BS in the remaining categories, in- creasing the impacts in TA (192.49% and 178.91%), FE (139.85% and 187.10%), and FRS (162% and 254.50%). In WC, while S1 decreased the consumption by 44.18%, S2 increased water use by 60.06% when compared with BS.

About the analysis of contributions, the animal breeding was the main unit process with a very similar behavior in both scenarios in CC (S1 and S2, 97.70%), TA (S1, 95.15%; S2, 92.09%), FE (S1, 79.37%; S2, 82.77%), LU (S1 and S2, 99.70%), and WC (S1, 85.17%; S2, 93.10%).

In CC, the contribution of CH4 and N2O from the enteric fermentation and manure is the main elementary flow in both S1 and S2 -87%). Feed supplementation contributed 9.94% in S1 and 10.56% in S2, being the maize grain (organic and nonorganic), the soybean (organic and nonorganic), and the transports as the most relevant inputs for CC category.

Regarding the GHG emissions from land use change (land transformation), the contributions of S1 and S2 were 0.56% and 0.60%, respectively, being the feed supplementation (mainly the NPK production) and paper towel the most rele- vant processes.
In addition, the higher milk production per dairy buffalo in the S1 and S2 resulted in a lower methane rate per kilogram of FPCM, which also contributed to fewer impacts in CC. Bartl

et al. (2011) and Bakken et al. (2017) made a similar obser- vation in studying a dairy cow and in assessing the impacts of the intensification in cow milk production. The authors veri- fied that a decrease in the enteric methane emissions followed an increase in milk production per cow, mainly because of the dilution of the emissions per unit of milk produced. Thus, the use of concentrate feed allowed the increase in animal yield, which contributed to lower methane emission rates.

In TA, the feed supplementation contributed 85.98% and 77.09% to S1 and S2, respectively, mainly because of the maize grain, heat, transports, and mineral fertilizers (NPK) used in the agriculture cultivation.

Regarding FE, the feed supplementation showed the main impacts in both S1 and S2, with 79.32% and 82.73%, because of the production of paper towel used in the teats cleaning (wastewater and solid treatments) and the maize and soybean grain production.

In LU, the occupation of natural grassland area for live- stock grazing highlighted with 99.22% in S1 and S2. Although the feed supplementation requires an agricultural area to produce grains, the reduction in 60% of pasture area per animal (1 ha to 0.4 ha) ensured the relevant reduction in LU compared with BS.

Related to FRS, the feed supplementation contributed with 81.12% in S1 and 86.04% in S2 because of the fossil fuels used in the transports (~30.50%) and agricultural services to produce grains, mainly maize (~40%). The mineral fertilizer production (urea) relevantly contributed ~ 18% in both S1 and S2 scenarios. The mineral extraction and the use of fertilizers and pesticides in nonorganic agricultural practices showed an important hotspot to use fossil resources. Although the organ- ic grain production does not require synthetic fertilizers and pesticides, it uses other agricultural inputs, such as organic green manure for soil fertilization that also results in environ- mental impacts in this category.

Lastly, in WC, the water used in the milk production and cleaning majorly contributed (57.54%) in S1. Feed supple- mentation was the main contributor in WC (66.35%) in S2, mainly because of the irrigation in the maize grain production (63.50%). The higher water consumption in organic crops compared with the nonorganics (e.g., the soybean organic requires 26 times more water than soybean non-organic) caused the difference between S1 and S2.

40: In S3 and S4, the semi-intensive system for buffalo milk from S1 and S2 was modified by adding whey and, consequently, partially replacing the feed supplementation with organic (S3) and nonorganic (S4).
In all categories, S3 presented a slightly reduction com- pared with S1 (CC, 1.24%; TA, 13.17%; FE, 8.89%; LU, 0.05%; FRS, 10.41%; and WC, 2.65%). This behavior was
the same between S4 and S2 (CC, 1.22%; TA, 8.89%; FE, 10.42%; LU, 0.06%; FRS, 9.19%; and WC, 8.40%).
Regarding the GHG emission from land use change, both S3 and S4 showed no differences compared with S1 and S2, respectively.

Whey in the animals' diets is especially useful in dry sea- sons or with animals only raised on pastures with low protein content (David et al. 2010). Besides the benefits of the agri- cultural phase of milk production, because of less maize con- sumption, reducing the environmental impacts related to dairy products is still possible. Whey is considered the main effluent from cheese production and can cause negative environmental impacts when not properly treated (Palmieri et al. 2016). The results of this study are consistent with Flysjo et al. (2014), who stated that using whey to feed animals potentially con- tributes to avoiding some relevant environmental impacts.

Economic allocation obtained the former results in this work, considering the whey with no commercial value (0% allocation). For mass allocation, whey contributed between 80 and 90% since the amount of whey produced is related to the productivity of cheese (Prazeres et al. 2012). For an allocation based on the nutritional percentage for human consumption, whey should contribute with 50%, which corresponds to its content of soluble protein, lactose, vitamins, and minerals (Bylund 2003). In this work, the whey used in animal feed as a substitute for maize corresponded to about 24% in energy value. For economic allocation, considering a minimum value to the whey is necessary SO that its upstream impacts can be accounted for.

Thus, a sensitivity analysis was performed, ranging the contribution of whey's impact from 0 to 100%, with 20% steps, considering the possible allocations. The sensitivity analysis showed that for the 20%, the impacts remained close to all categories in S3. In S4, LU and WC were indifferent to the change of allocation and CC, which presented a small difference compared with TA, FE, and FRS categories. Thus, whey has 20% of the economic value of cheese.

43: S5 and S6 assessed the intensive system for buffalo milk pro- duction. Compared with BS, the confined handling systems S5 and S6 reduced the impacts in CC (S5A, 27.21%; S6A, 22.99%; S5B, 35.89%; and S6B, 29.82%) and LU (S5A and S5B, 0.45%; S6A and S6B, 0.38%), especially because of the high milk production. Other aspects such as the consumption of feeds with high digestibility and the reduction of livestock area contributed to the impact in CC and LU, respectively.

The impacts increased in the remaining categories to all scenarios considered when the system changed from exten- sive (BS) to intensive (S5 and S6) handling. These increases were higher than 450% in TA, 750% in FE, 320% in FRS, and 120% in WC.

The unit process animal breeding was the most impactful in S5 and S6 among all categories. In CC, its contribution was ~ 96% in S5A and S6A and ~ 97% in S5B and S6B because of the direct emissions and feed supplementation (S5A, 73.72% and 23.16%; S6A, 71.07% and 25.27%; S5B, 55.88% and 41.76%; S6B, 54.79% and 42.39%, respectively). The GHG emissions from land use change in both S5B and S6B present- ed no differences in absolute values and a slight decrease in the relative contribution analysis (around 0.20%) as compared with S5A and S6A. As compared with BS, the GHG from land use change reduced in S5A and S5B (11%) as well as in S6A and S6B (6%).

In LU, animal breeding had an impact of 90.13% in S5A, 88.44% in S6A, 99.19% in S5B, and 88.51% in S6B. The main contributors to the category were the area occupied for animal confinement and feed supplementations (S5A, 69.95% and 20.17%; S6A, 62.93% and 25.50%; S5B, 69.53 and 20.65%; S6B, 62.56% and 25.95%, respectively).

In TA, animal breeding had an impact of 97% in S5A and S6A and 98% in S5B and S6B. The main contributor was the feed supplementation (~93% in S5A and S6A, ~95% S5B and S6B). In FE, animal breeding had an impact of 94% in S5A, S6A, S5B, and S6B. Feed supplementation con- tributed with ~ 94% in all scenarios. The highest impacts at- tributed to TA and FE in S5A and S6A were due to the use of corn silage as a fiber source in the animal feed. Sabia et al. (2018) state that maize silage requires high amounts of urea and extensive use of machines.
In FRS, animal breeding had an impact of ~ 87% in S5A and S6A and ~ 96% in S5B and S6B. Feed supplementation contributed 87% in S5A and S6A and ~ 96% in S5B and S6B.

In WC, animal breeding had an impact of 93% in S5A, S6A, S5B, and S6B. The main contributors in this category were the feed supplementation and animal watering (S5A, 74.69% and 18.34%; S6A, 79.60% and 13.63%; S5B, 74.99% and 18.13%; S6B, 79.78% and 13.51%, respectively).

Thus, the impacts from the confined system were mainly due to the feed supplementation as pointed out by Pirlo et al. (2014a). Grain cultivation requires inputs such as NPK fertilizers, agri- cultural pesticides, genetically modified seeds, irrigation of large areas, and agricultural practices. Such practices include the use of intensive mechanization with consumption of fossil fuels, especially oil and diesel. Therefore, intensive farming practices can increase environmental impacts in specific environmental categories as compared with the extensive BS system.

Although the confined system has been more productive than semi-intensive and extensive systems, it resulted in great- er impacts at specific stages of the milk life cycle, such as animal breeding. However, livestock farming in a confined system avoids the use of large areas for pasture, reducing its transformation and occupation on the livestock farm stage. In the confined system, the land occupation was reduced

expressively, even considering the areas occupied by the ani- mal and feed crop production (0.45% in S5 and 0.38% in S6). This shows that for LU, the intensive system is an environ- mentally advantageous scenario.

The sensitivity analysis for the intensive system showed that, except for LU, the transport of feed was relevant in all impact categories. The purchasing of feed near the dairy farm is then vital in the sustainability of intensive systems. Thus, the intensification of buffalo milk production can benefit or harm the environment, depending on particular farming sys- tems and practices.

In LU, animal breeding had an impact of 90.13% in S5A, 88.44% in S6A, 99.19% in S5B, and 88.51% in S6B. The main contributors to the category were the area occupied for animal confinement and feed supplementations (S5A, 69.95% and 20.17%; S6A, 62.93% and 25.50%; S5B, 69.53 and 20.65%; S6B, 62.56% and 25.95%, respectively).

In TA, animal breeding had an impact of 97% in S5A and S6A and 98% in S5B and S6B. The main contributor was the feed supplementation (~93% in S5A and S6A, ~95% S5B and S6B). In FE, animal breeding had an impact of 94% in S5A, S6A, S5B, and S6B. Feed supplementation con- tributed with ~ 94% in all scenarios. The highest impacts at- tributed to TA and FE in S5A and S6A were due to the use of corn silage as a fiber source in the animal feed. Sabia et al. (2018) state that maize silage requires high amounts of urea and extensive use of machines.
In FRS, animal breeding had an impact of ~ 87% in S5A and S6A and ~ 96% in S5B and S6B. Feed supplementation contributed 87% in S5A and S6A and ~ 96% in S5B and S6B.

In WC, animal breeding had an impact of 93% in S5A, S6A, S5B, and S6B. The main contributors in this category were the feed supplementation and animal watering (S5A, 74.69% and 18.34%; S6A, 79.60% and 13.63%; S5B, 74.99% and 18.13%; S6B, 79.78% and 13.51%, respectively).

Thus, the impacts from the confined system were mainly due to the feed supplementation as pointed out by Pirlo et al. (2014a). Grain cultivation requires inputs such as NPK fertilizers, agri- cultural pesticides, genetically modified seeds, irrigation of large areas, and agricultural practices. Such practices include the use of intensive mechanization with consumption of fossil fuels, especially oil and diesel. Therefore, intensive farming practices can increase environmental impacts in specific environmental categories as compared with the extensive BS system.

Although the confined system has been more productive than semi-intensive and extensive systems, it resulted in great- er impacts at specific stages of the milk life cycle, such as animal breeding. However, livestock farming in a confined system avoids the use of large areas for pasture, reducing its transformation and occupation on the livestock farm stage. In the confined system, the land occupation was reduced

expressively, even considering the areas occupied by the ani- mal and feed crop production (0.45% in S5 and 0.38% in S6). This shows that for LU, the intensive system is an environ- mentally advantageous scenario.

The sensitivity analysis for the intensive system showed that, except for LU, the transport of feed was relevant in all impact categories. The purchasing of feed near the dairy farm is then vital in the sustainability of intensive systems. Thus, the intensification of buffalo milk production can benefit or harm the environment, depending on particular farming sys- tems and practices.

Other aspects such as the consumption of feeds with high digestibility and the reduction of livestock area contributed to the impact in CC and LU, respectively.

48: The scenarios proposed for studying the buffalo milk produc- tion systems allowed an integrated view of the impacts related to the intensification of the production and the choice for an organic or nonorganic handling system.

The intensification of buffalo milk production has in- creased the impact related to terrestrial acidification, freshwa- ter eutrophication, and fossil resource scarcity and decreased the impact related to land use, water consumption, and climate change.

Changing from organic to conventional livestock system led to a trend toward increased impacts in terrestrial acidifica- tion, freshwater eutrophication, land use, and water consump- tion. Regarding "climate change" and "fossil resource scarcity," pointing out a trend is not enough. However, the intensification of production should be achieved by improving both the quality of animal feeding and the management of cropping practices.

Besides the environmental gains, the use of whey in animal feed may also result in low cost of the diets. Moreover, the disposal and transport of whey to the waste treatment plants will not more required.

On the other hand, the results also confirm that the replac- ing part of supplements by whey reduces the impacts in ter- restrial acidification, water consumption, and fossil resource scarcity. For the "climate change" and "land occupation," the differences were less obvious.
This study also contributes to the datasets for life cycle in- ventory of buffalo milk, being the first one performed in Brazil.

50: The scenarios proposed for studying the buffalo milk produc- tion systems allowed an integrated view of the impacts related to the intensification of the production and the choice for an organic or nonorganic handling system.

The intensification of buffalo milk production has in- creased the impact related to terrestrial acidification, freshwa- ter eutrophication, and fossil resource scarcity and decreased the impact related to land use, water consumption, and climate change.

Changing from organic to conventional livestock system led to a trend toward increased impacts in terrestrial acidifica- tion, freshwater eutrophication, land use, and water consump- tion. Regarding "climate change" and "fossil resource scarcity," pointing out a trend is not enough. However, the intensification of production should be achieved by improving both the quality of animal feeding and the management of cropping practices.

Besides the environmental gains, the use of whey in animal feed may also result in low cost of the diets. Moreover, the disposal and transport of whey to the waste treatment plants will not more required.

On the other hand, the results also confirm that the replac- ing part of supplements by whey reduces the impacts in ter- restrial acidification, water consumption, and fossil resource scarcity. For the "climate change" and "land occupation," the differences were less obvious.
This study also contributes to the datasets for life cycle in- ventory of buffalo milk, being the first one performed in Brazil.

53: The scenarios proposed for studying the buffalo milk produc- tion systems allowed an integrated view of the impacts related to the intensification of the production and the choice for an organic or nonorganic handling system.

The intensification of buffalo milk production has in- creased the impact related to terrestrial acidification, freshwa- ter eutrophication, and fossil resource scarcity and decreased the impact related to land use, water consumption, and climate change.

Changing from organic to conventional livestock system led to a trend toward increased impacts in terrestrial acidifica- tion, freshwater eutrophication, land use, and water consump- tion. Regarding "climate change" and "fossil resource scarcity," pointing out a trend is not enough. However, the intensification of production should be achieved by improving both the quality of animal feeding and the management of cropping practices.

Besides the environmental gains, the use of whey in animal feed may also result in low cost of the diets. Moreover, the disposal and transport of whey to the waste treatment plants will not more required.

On the other hand, the results also confirm that the replac- ing part of supplements by whey reduces the impacts in ter- restrial acidification, water consumption, and fossil resource scarcity. For the "climate change" and "land occupation," the differences were less obvious.
This study also contributes to the datasets for life cycle in- ventory of buffalo milk, being the first one performed in Brazil.
